people.text: Emma wants a friendly book club where members discuss modern novels. She can only attend on weekday evenings and prefers meetings where people do not need to finish a whole book every time.
Lucas enjoys crime fiction and wants lively online discussions in a small group because he travels often for work.
Maya is improving her English and wants short books with vocabulary help. She is free on Saturday mornings and feels nervous speaking.
Oliver wants serious discussions about classic literature with experienced readers. He can attend meetings in the city centre after work.
Sofia wants to meet other parents and read books about family life. She needs somewhere children can stay safely nearby.

texts.text: Parents’ Pages meets in a community centre beside a supervised playroom. Members read novels connected with [10]family relationships[/10], and [10]children can stay safely nearby during meetings[/10].
History Readers focuses on biographies and historical non-fiction. Meetings usually last three hours and include guest speakers.

Saturday Stories is for lower-intermediate English learners. Members read [8]short easy books[/8], receive [8]help with difficult vocabulary[/8] and meet on Saturday mornings. The group leader encourages shy students to join discussions confidently.
Weekend Comics Club is for teenagers who enjoy graphic novels, manga and drawing activities.

Page Turners meets every Wednesday evening in a small café near the station. Members discuss [6]recent novels[/6], but meetings focus on [6]selected chapters rather than whole books[/6]. New members are welcomed warmly and discussions are relaxed.

Mystery Circle is designed for fans of detective stories. The club meets [7]online twice a month[/7] and limits sessions to [7]twelve members so everyone can speak[/7]. Members discuss famous crime writers and surprising endings.
Poetry Exchange welcomes university students interested in writing and performing original poems for competitions.
The Classics Forum meets in the central library on Thursday evenings. It is aimed at confident readers who enjoy detailed conversations about older literature and literary history.

answers.explanation: Emma needs weekday evening discussions about modern novels without full-book reading.
Lucas wants online crime-fiction discussions in a small group.
Maya needs easy books, vocabulary support and Saturday morning meetings.
Oliver wants serious classic literature discussions after work.
Sofia needs family-themed books and childcare nearby.
